The output files were stored in:

11_BlastX_GenRefCycas_Zamia_RNA/

13.1 BLASTx command parameters

The BLASTx searches were executed using the following key parameters:

  • -db cycas_pep_db: Custom BLAST protein database generated from the Cycas panzhihuaensis proteome.
  • -max_target_seqs 1: Retains only the best match for each transcript.
  • -evalue 1e-25: Stringent significance threshold.
  • -num_threads: Number of CPU threads allocated by SLURM.
  • -outfmt 6: Standard tabular BLAST output format.

Unlike the searches against NR, this analysis focused exclusively on identifying the best homologous protein in the reference cycad proteome.

13.2 Protein coverage assessment

Following BLASTx, the script analyze_blastPlus_topHit_coverage.pl from the Trinity package was used to evaluate reference protein coverage.

This analysis estimates:

  • The proportion of reference proteins represented in the transcriptome assembly.
  • The fraction of each protein covered by assembled transcripts.
  • The completeness of reconstructed coding sequences.
  • The distribution of full-length and partial transcript matches.

These metrics provide an additional measure of transcriptome quality and gene-space recovery.

13.3 SLURM script used for BLASTx against the Cycas reference proteome

SLURM script: 11_Blastx_REFcycas_proteins_Zamia_RNA.sh 
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J BlxRefCy_Za
#SBATCH -o blastxRefCy_Zamia-%j.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --array=1-12
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=10G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca


######### 
module load trinity/2.15.2
module load  ncbiblast/2.16.0

######### Rutas generales

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
trinity_base="${base_dir}/03_trinity_separated"
blastx_base="${base_dir}/11_BlastX_GenRefCycas_Zamia_RNA"
pep_ref="${base_dir}/Cycas_panzhihuaensis_genome.pep/Cycas_panzhihuaensis_genome.pep"

######### Obtener muestra

sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)
trinity_dir=$(find "$trinity_base" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_trinity" | sort | tail -n 1)
fasta_file=$(find "$trinity_dir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name "*.Trinity.fasta" | head -n 1)

######### Crear carpeta de salida

output_dir="${blastx_base}/${sample}_${fecha}_blastx"
mkdir -p "$output_dir"
log_file="${output_dir}/${sample}_blastx.log"

######### Crear base de datos BLAST si no existe
# Para proteinas dbtype prot
# Para nucleotidos dbtype nucl

makeblastdb -in "$pep_ref" -dbtype prot -parse_seqids -out "${output_dir}/cycas_pep_db" >> "$log_file" 2>&1


######### Exporta la ruta donde est  tu NR, base de datos taxonomicos 

export BLASTDB=/biodata/blast

######### Ejecutar BLASTx contra cycas_pep_db

blastx -query "$fasta_file" \
  -db "${output_dir}/cycas_pep_db" \
  -max_target_seqs 1 \
  -evalue 1e-25 \
  -num_threads "$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK" \
  -outfmt 6 \
  -out "${output_dir}/${sample}_blastx_topHit.outfmt6" \
  >> "$log_file" 2>&1

######### Analizar cobertura con Trinity

coverage_script="/prg/trinityrnaseq/2.15.2/util/analyze_blastPlus_topHit_coverage.pl"

"$coverage_script" \
  "${output_dir}/${sample}_blastx_topHit.outfmt6" \
  "$pep_ref" \
  "$pep_ref" >> "$log_file" 2>&1

echo "BLASTx y an lisis de cobertura completado para $sample"

13.4 Output files

11_BlastX_GenRefCycas_Zamia_RNA/
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_blastx/
│   ├── cycas_pep_db.*
│   ├── JCV23-0101_blastx_topHit.outfmt6
│   ├── JCV23-0101_blastx.log
│   ├── blastx_topHit_coverage_report.txt
│   └── ...
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_blastx/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_blastx/
└── ...

The resulting BLAST and coverage statistics were subsequently used to evaluate the representation of conserved cycad genes within the Zamia transcriptome assemblies and to identify transcripts corresponding to near full-length protein-coding genes.

14 Coverage-Based and Taxonomic Filtering of Transcript Assemblies

Following transcriptome assembly and similarity searches against the Cycas panzhihuaensis reference proteome, a multi-step filtering strategy was applied to retain high-confidence plant transcripts while removing poorly supported or potentially contaminant sequences.

The filtering workflow consisted of three sequential steps:

  1. Selection of transcripts with high protein coverage against the Cycas reference proteome.
  2. Taxonomic assignment of the retained transcripts using BlobTools2 and BLASTx results.
  3. Extraction of transcripts assigned to Viridiplantae for downstream analyses.

14.1 Filtering Transcripts by Reference Protein Coverage

The first filtering step retained only transcripts showing substantial alignment coverage against proteins from Cycas panzhihuaensis.

Coverage information was obtained from the file generated by Trinity:

*.w_pct_hit_length

Only transcripts covering at least 80% of the length of their best reference protein hit were retained.

This threshold was chosen to enrich the dataset for nearly complete coding sequences and reduce the number of fragmented transcript assemblies.

14.1.1 Coverage filtering criterion

The filtering was based on:

  • Column 14 (pct_hit_len_aligned) from the Trinity coverage report.
  • Minimum coverage threshold: ≥ 80%.
  • Transcript extraction performed using seqtk subseq.

14.1.2 SLURM script used for coverage filtering

SLURM script: 12_1_filter_cover_Zamia.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J filterCov
#SBATCH -o filterCov_ZamRNA-%j.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --array=1-12
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --mem=5G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca

set -euo pipefail

################

module load trinity/2.15.2
module load ncbiblast/2.16.0
module load seqtk/1.2

################
# Rutas base

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
trinity_base="${base_dir}/03_trinity_separated"
blastx_base="${base_dir}/10_BlastX_GenRefCycas_Zamia_RNA"

# Carpeta de salida de los FASTA filtrados por cobertura

filter_root="${base_dir}/11_Filter_topHit_coverage_Zamia_RNA"
mkdir -p "$filter_root"

################
# Extraer la muestra y fecha

sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)

################
# Localizar archivos

trinity_dir=$(find "$trinity_base" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_trinity" | sort | tail -n 1)
fasta_file=$(find "$trinity_dir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name "*.Trinity.fasta" | head -n 1)

blastx_dir=$(find "$blastx_base" -maxdepth 1 -type d \
             -name "${sample}_*_blastx" | sort | tail -n1)

if [[ -z "$blastx_dir" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: No encontr  ning n directorio *${sample}_*_blastx en $blastx_base" >&2
  exit 1
fi

coverage_file="${blastx_dir}/${sample}_blastx_topHit.outfmt6.w_pct_hit_length"

if [[ ! -f "$coverage_file" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: No se encontr  el archivo de cobertura (.w_pct_hit_length):"
  echo "  $coverage_file" >&2
  exit 1
fi

################
# Preparamos carpeta y log de filtrado

filter_dir="${filter_root}/${sample}_${fecha}_filtered_hitsCover"
mkdir -p "$filter_dir"
log_file="${filter_dir}/${sample}_filterCoverage.log"

# Redirigimos stdout y stderr a log (y pantalla)
exec > >(tee -i "$log_file") 2>&1

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Filtrando transcritos con cobertura = 80 % para $sample"

################
# Extraer los IDs con cobertura = 80 % (columna 14 = pct_hit_len_aligned)

awk '$14 >= 80 { print $1 }' "$coverage_file" \
  > "${filter_dir}/${sample}_80cov.ids"

n_ids=$(wc -l < "${filter_dir}/${sample}_80cov.ids")
echo "Encontrados $n_ids transcritos con = 80 % de cobertura."

################
# Sub-secuenciar el FASTA original

seqtk subseq "$fasta_file" "${filter_dir}/${sample}_80cov.ids" \
  > "${filter_dir}/${sample}_80cov.fasta"

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Filtrado completado."
echo "Resultado: ${filter_dir}/${sample}_80cov.fasta"

14.1.3 Output files

11_Filter_topHit_coverage_Zamia_RNA/
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_filtered_hitsCover/
│   ├── JCV23-0101_80cov.ids
│   ├── JCV23-0101_80cov.fasta
│   └── JCV23-0101_filterCoverage.log
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_filtered_hitsCover/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_filtered_hitsCover/
└── ...

The resulting FASTA files contain transcripts supported by strong protein-level evidence from a closely related cycad species.

14.2 Taxonomic Assignment of High-Coverage Transcripts

The filtered transcript assemblies were subsequently analyzed using BlobTools2.

A new BlobDir database was generated from each filtered FASTA file and populated with taxonomic information derived from the original BLASTx searches against the NR database.

This step allowed the assignment of each retained transcript to a taxonomic lineage.

Input files included:

  • Filtered FASTA files from Step 1.
  • BLASTx results against NR115.
  • NCBI taxonomy database (taxdump).

14.2.1 BlobTools2 taxonomic assignment parameters

The following parameters were used:

  • --hits: BLASTx results against NR115.
  • --taxdump: NCBI taxonomy database.
  • --taxrule bestsumorder: BlobTools2 taxonomic assignment algorithm.
  • --evalue 1e-25: Minimum significance threshold.
  • --replace: Replaces previous taxonomic assignments if present.

14.2.1.1 Taxonomic assignment method

BlobTools2 uses the bestsumorder algorithm, which assigns taxonomy by evaluating all significant BLAST hits associated with a transcript and selecting the most supported taxonomic lineage.

This approach is generally more robust than relying solely on the top BLAST hit and reduces misclassification caused by isolated matches.

14.2.2 SLURM script used for taxonomic assignment

SLURM script: 12_2_Blob_CreAdd_filter_Cover_Zamia.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J Blob-filter
#SBATCH -o BlobFilter_ZamRNA-%j.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --array=1-12
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --mem=15G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca



################
module load miniconda/3
module load blobtools/2
module load python/3.11
module load seqtk/1.2

#Activate conda environment before running
eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate btk_env

#### Rutas base

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
filter_root="$base_dir/11_Filter_topHit_coverage_Zamia_RNA"
blastx_base="$base_dir/06_BlastX_Zamia_RNA"
taxdump_dir="$base_dir/new_taxdump"
blobtools_root="$base_dir/12_BlobTools2_FilterCoverage_Zamia_RNA"

mkdir -p "$blobtools_root"

#### Muestra y fecha
sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)

#### Localizar FASTA filtrado (cobertura = 80 %)

filter_dir="$filter_root/${sample}_${fecha}_filtered_hitsCover"
filtered_fasta="$filter_dir/${sample}_80cov.fasta"

if [[ ! -f "$filtered_fasta" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: FASTA filtrado no encontrado:" >&2
  echo "  $filtered_fasta" >&2
  exit 1
fi

################
#  Preparar BlobDir y log en la salida de la muestra

blobdir_out="$blobtools_root/${sample}_${fecha}_filteredBlobDir"
mkdir -p "$blobdir_out"

log_file="$blobdir_out/${sample}_blobtools.log"
exec > >(tee -i "$log_file") 2>&1
echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Iniciando BlobTools2 para $sample"


################
# Crear BlobDir a partir del FASTA filtrado

/prg/blobtools/2/blobtools2/blobtools create \
  --fasta "$filtered_fasta" \
  "$blobdir_out"

###############
# Subconjuntar hits NR115 originales

nr_dir=$(find "$blastx_base" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_blastx_nr115" | tail -n1)
hit_file="$nr_dir/${sample}_blastx.outfmt6"

if [[ ! -f "$hit_file" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: Hits NR115 no encontrados:" >&2
  echo "  $hit_file" >&2
  exit 1
fi

#### 2.1) Subconjuntar los hits de NR115 a los contigs de =80 %  
ids_file="$filter_dir/${sample}_80cov.ids"  
filtered_hits="$blobdir_out/${sample}_blastx_NR115_filtered.outfmt6"

grep -F -w -f "$ids_file" "$hit_file" > "$filtered_hits"  
echo "    Hits de NR115 reducidos a $(wc -l < "$filtered_hits") contigs filtrados"


################
# A adir clasificaci n taxon mica de NR115 al BlobDir

/prg/blobtools/2/blobtools2/blobtools add \
  --hits "$filtered_hits" \
  --hits-cols 1=qseqid,2=staxids,3=bitscore,4=sseqid,5=sscinames,6=pident,7=length,8=mismatch,9=gapopen,10=qstart,11=qend,12=sstart,13=send,14=evalue \
  --taxdump    "$taxdump_dir" \
  --taxrule    bestsumorder \
  --evalue 1e-25 \
  --replace \
  "$blobdir_out"

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] BlobTools2 completado para $sample"
conda deactivate

14.2.3 Output files

12_BlobTools2_FilterCoverage_Zamia_RNA/
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_filteredBlobDir/
│   ├── meta.json
│   ├── identifiers.json
│   ├── summary.json
│   ├── JCV23-0101_blastx_NR115_filtered.outfmt6
│   └── ...
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_filteredBlobDir/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_filteredBlobDir/
└── ...

14.3 Extraction of Viridiplantae Transcripts

The final filtering step retained only transcripts assigned to Viridiplantae.

Using BlobTools2, all transcripts classified outside the plant kingdom were excluded. The resulting identifiers were then used to extract the corresponding sequences from the filtered FASTA files.

This procedure generated the final set of transcripts used in downstream analyses.

14.3.1 BlobTools2 filtering parameters

The filtering was based on:

  • Taxonomic rank: Kingdom.
  • Target group: Viridiplantae.
  • Taxonomic assignments generated using the bestsumorder algorithm.

14.3.1.1 Why filter by Viridiplantae?

Although transcriptomes are derived from plant tissues, RNA extractions frequently contain transcripts originating from:

  • Endophytic fungi.
  • Epiphytic microorganisms.
  • Bacteria.
  • Viruses.
  • Environmental contaminants.

Retaining only sequences assigned to Viridiplantae increases confidence that downstream analyses focus on host plant transcripts.

14.3.2 SLURM script used for Viridiplantae filtering

SLURM script: 12_3_Blob_filter_Streptophyta_Zamia.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J Blob-filter
#SBATCH -o BlobFilter_ZamRNA-%j.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --array=1-12
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --mem=15G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca

##############################################
# 1. Activar entorno y cargar módulos
##############################################

module load miniconda/3
module load blobtools/2
module load python/3.11
module load seqtk/1.2

#Activate conda environment before running
eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate btk_env

##############################################
# 2. Rutas base
##############################################

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
blobtools_root="${base_dir}/12_BlobTools2_FilterCoverage_Zamia_RNA"
filter_root="${base_dir}/11_Filter_topHit_coverage_Zamia_RNA"
filtered_output_root="${base_dir}/13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA"

mkdir -p "$filtered_output_root"

##############################################
# 3. Muestra y fecha
##############################################

sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)

##############################################
# 4. Rutas específicas
##############################################
# Localizar el BlobDir (ignorar fecha)
blobdir_in=$(find "$blobtools_root" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_filteredBlobDir" | sort | tail -n 1)

# Localizar el directorio del FASTA filtrado por cobertura (ignorar fecha)
filter_dir=$(find "$filter_root" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_filtered_hitsCover" | sort | tail -n 1)
filtered_fasta=$(find "$filter_dir" -type f -name "${sample}_80cov.fasta" | head -n 1)

# Definir carpeta de salida
filtered_out="${filtered_output_root}/${sample}_${fecha}_filtered_Viridiplantae"

log_file="${filtered_out}/${sample}_blobtools_filter.log"
mkdir -p "$filtered_out"

##############################################
# 5. Iniciar log
##############################################

exec > >(tee -i "$log_file") 2>&1
echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Iniciando filtro para conservar solo Viridiplantae en $sample"
echo "BlobDir In: $blobdir_in"
echo "Filtered FASTA: $filtered_fasta"
echo "Filtered Out Dir: $filtered_out"

##############################################
# 6. Ejecutar filtro por Viridiplantae
##############################################

/prg/blobtools/2/blobtools2/blobtools filter \
  --param bestsumorder_kingdom--Keys=Viridiplantae \
  --invert \
  --fasta "$filtered_fasta" \
  --summary "$filtered_out/summary.json" \
  --table "$filtered_out/table.tsv" \
  --output "$filtered_out" \
  "$blobdir_in"

##############################################
# 7. Extraer lista de IDs desde identifiers.json
##############################################

id_json="$filtered_out/identifiers.json"
id_txt="$filtered_out/${sample}_Viridiplantae_ids.txt"
fasta_out="$filtered_out/${sample}_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta"

if [[ ! -f "$id_json" ]]; then
  echo "[ERROR] No se encontró el archivo identifiers.json" >&2
  exit 1
fi

python3 -c "
import json
with open('$id_json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
with open('$id_txt', 'w') as out:
    for identifier in data.get('values', []):
        out.write(identifier + '\n')
"

if [[ ! -s "$id_txt" ]]; then
  echo "[ERROR] El archivo de IDs está vacío o falló la conversión." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "  • $(wc -l < "$id_txt") identificadores extraídos a $id_txt"

##############################################
# 8. Extraer secuencias FASTA
##############################################

seqtk subseq "$filtered_fasta" "$id_txt" > "$fasta_out"

if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "[ERROR] Falló la extracción de secuencias con seqtk." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "  • FASTA filtrado generado en $fasta_out"
echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Proceso completado para $sample"

conda deactivate

14.3.3 Output files

13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA/
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_filtered_Viridiplantae/
│   ├── identifiers.json
│   ├── summary.json
│   ├── table.tsv
│   ├── JCV23-0101_Viridiplantae_ids.txt
│   ├── JCV23-0101_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta
│   └── JCV23-0101_blobtools_filter.log
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_filtered_Viridiplantae/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_filtered_Viridiplantae/
└── ...

list of output files BlobTools2 Filter.

14.3.4 Final Output

The final FASTA files generated in this step represent a curated transcriptome dataset composed of:

  • Transcripts with strong protein-level support (≥80% coverage).
  • Sequences with significant similarity to known proteins.
  • Transcripts taxonomically assigned to Viridiplantae.

These filtered assemblies were used as the starting point for downstream functional annotation and transcriptome analyses.

15 TransRate Assessment of Filtered Transcriptomes

After coverage-based and taxonomic filtering, the final Viridiplantae transcriptomes were evaluated using TransRate. This analysis assesses transcriptome quality by mapping the original RNA-seq reads back to the assembled transcripts and calculating several assembly support metrics.

Unlike BUSCO, which evaluates completeness using conserved orthologs, TransRate evaluates how well the sequencing reads support the assembled transcripts.

The analysis was performed using:

  • Filtered Viridiplantae transcriptomes generated by BlobTools2.
  • Original cleaned paired-end reads generated after Trimmomatic filtering.

Input files were obtained from:

13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA/

The resulting TransRate reports were stored in:

14_Transrate_FilteredFASTA/

15.1 TransRate quality metrics

TransRate calculates several assembly-support statistics, including:

  • Read mapping rate.
  • Contig coverage.
  • Contig score.
  • Assembly score.
  • Number of supported transcripts.
  • Number of poorly supported transcripts.

These metrics help identify assembly artifacts and provide an independent evaluation of transcriptome quality.

15.1.1 Interpretation of TransRate scores

Important metrics to consider include:

Metric Interpretation
Assembly score Overall quality of the transcriptome assembly
Mapping rate Percentage of reads that align back to assembled transcripts
Good contigs Number of transcripts strongly supported by reads
Low-supported contigs Potential assembly artifacts or low-confidence transcripts

Higher assembly scores and mapping rates generally indicate more reliable transcriptome assemblies.

15.2 SLURM script used for TransRate analysis

SLURM script: 13_TransRate_FilterZamia_RNA.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J TraRate_Zam
#SBATCH -o TransRate_Zam_test.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --array=1-5,7-12
#SBATCH --mem=10G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca


##############################################
# 1. Cargar módulos necesarios
##############################################

module load usearch/9.2.64_i86linux32
module load bowtie/2.3.4.1
module load eXpress/1.5.1
module load salmon/0.11.0
module load transrate/1.0.3

##############################################
# 2. Definir rutas base
##############################################

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
reads_base="${base_dir}/01_clean_reads_Zamia_RNA_trimmomatic"
filtered_fasta_base="${base_dir}/13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA"
results_base="${base_dir}/14_Transrate_FilteredFASTA"

mkdir -p "$results_base"

##############################################
# 3. Obtener nombre de muestra
##############################################

sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)

##############################################
# 4. Localizar archivo FASTA filtrado por reino
##############################################

filtered_fasta_dir=$(find "$filtered_fasta_base" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_filtered_Viridiplantae*" | sort | tail -n 1)
filtered_fasta=$(find "$filtered_fasta_dir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name "${sample}_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta" | head -n 1)

if [[ ! -f "$filtered_fasta" ]]; then
  echo "[ERROR] No se encontró el archivo FASTA filtrado por reino para $sample" >&2
  exit 1
fi

##############################################
# 5. Definir archivos de lectura y salida
##############################################

left_reads="${reads_base}/${sample}/${sample}_R1_paired.fastq.gz"
right_reads="${reads_base}/${sample}/${sample}_R2_paired.fastq.gz"
output_dir="${results_base}/${sample}_${fecha}_transrate_filtered"
log_file="${output_dir}/${sample}_transrate_filtered.log"

mkdir -p "$output_dir"

##############################################
# 6. Ejecutar Transrate con archivo filtrado
##############################################

transrate -a "$filtered_fasta" \
          --left "$left_reads" \
          --right "$right_reads" \
          -t 2 \
          --output "$output_dir" &> "$log_file"

echo "[INFO] Transrate completado para $sample"

15.3 Output files

14_Transrate_FilteredFASTA/
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_transrate_filtered/
│   ├── assemblies.csv
│   ├── contigs.csv
│   ├── scores.csv
│   ├── transrate.log
│   └── ...
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_transrate_filtered/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_transrate_filtered/
└── ...

The TransRate reports provide a final quality assessment of the filtered transcriptomes before coding-sequence prediction and functional annotation.

16 TransDecoder: Prediction of Coding Sequences

Following transcriptome quality assessment, TransDecoder was used to identify candidate coding regions (Open Reading Frames; ORFs) within the filtered Viridiplantae transcriptomes.

TransDecoder predicts likely protein-coding sequences by identifying long open reading frames and evaluating coding potential.

This step generates the protein datasets required for downstream functional annotation and orthology analyses.

Input files were obtained from:

13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA/

The predicted coding sequences were stored in:

15_TransDecoder_Zamia_RNA/

16.1 TransDecoder workflow

The analysis was performed in two stages:

16.1.1 TransDecoder.LongOrfs

Identifies candidate open reading frames within transcript sequences.

16.1.2 TransDecoder.Predict

Evaluates candidate ORFs and predicts the most likely coding regions.

16.2 Key output files

TransDecoder generates several useful files:

File Description
.pep Predicted protein sequences
.cds Predicted coding sequences
.gff3 Coordinates of predicted ORFs
.bed Genomic coordinates of ORFs

Among these, the protein FASTA (.pep) file is typically used for downstream annotation workflows.

16.3 SLURM script used for TransDecoder

SLURM script: 14_TransDecoder_Zamia_RNA.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J Tdecoder-zamia
#SBATCH -o TransDecoder_Zam_test.out
#SBATCH -p small
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=2
#SBATCH --array=1-12
#SBATCH --mem=10G
#SBATCH --time=1-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca


##############################################

module load transdecoder/5.5.0
module load usearch/9.2.64_i86linux32

##############################################
# 1. Rutas base
##############################################

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"

# Entrada: FASTA filtrado por BlobTools2
viridi_root="${base_dir}/13_BlobTools2_FilterKingdom_Zamia_RNA"

# Salida: carpeta final organizada
transdecoder_root="${base_dir}/15_TransDecoder_Zamia_RNA"
tabla_out="${transdecoder_root}/resumen_transdecoder.tsv"

mkdir -p "$transdecoder_root"

if [[ "$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID" == 1 && ! -f "$tabla_out" ]]; then
    echo -e "sample\ttotal_orfs\tmin_len\tmax_len\tavg_len" > "$tabla_out"
fi

##############################################
# 2. Identificar muestra
##############################################

sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
fecha=$(date +%Y%m%d)

viridi_dir=$(find "$viridi_root" -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "${sample}_*_filtered_Viridiplantae" | sort | tail -n 1)
fasta_in="${viridi_dir}/${sample}_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta"

if [[ ! -f "$fasta_in" ]]; then
    echo "[ERROR] FASTA no encontrado: $fasta_in"
    exit 1
fi

##############################################
# 3. Crear carpeta de trabajo y moverse all 
##############################################

td_out="${transdecoder_root}/${sample}_${fecha}_transdecoder_out"
mkdir -p "$td_out"
cd "$td_out" || exit 1

##############################################
# 4. Ejecutar TransDecoder desde output_dir
##############################################

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Procesando $sample"

TransDecoder.LongOrfs -t "$fasta_in"
TransDecoder.Predict -t "$fasta_in"

##############################################
# 5. Extraer m tricas del archivo .pep
##############################################

pep_file="${td_out}/$(basename "$fasta_in").transdecoder.pep"

if [[ ! -f "$pep_file" ]]; then
    echo "[ERROR] No se encontr  el archivo .pep para $sample"
    exit 1
fi

lens=($(awk '/^>/ {if (seq) print length(seq); seq=""} !/^>/ {seq=seq $0} END{print length(seq)}' "$pep_file"))

total=${#lens[@]}
min=${lens[0]}
max=${lens[0]}
sum=0

for len in "${lens[@]}"; do
    (( sum += len ))
    (( len < min )) && min=$len
    (( len > max )) && max=$len
done

avg=$(awk -v s=$sum -v t=$total 'BEGIN{printf "%.2f", s/t}')

# Escribir en la tabla resumen
{
    flock 200
    echo -e "${sample}\t${total}\t${min}\t${max}\t${avg}" >> "$tabla_out"
} 200>"${tabla_out}.lock"

echo " TransDecoder finalizado para $sample"

16.4 Summary statistics generated

For each sample, a summary table was generated containing:

  • Total number of predicted ORFs.
  • Minimum protein length.
  • Maximum protein length.
  • Mean protein length.

These metrics provide a general overview of the coding potential recovered from each transcriptome assembly.

16.5 Output files

15_TransDecoder_Zamia_RNA/
├── resumen_transdecoder.tsv
├── JCV23-0101_20250617_transdecoder_out/
│   ├── JCV23-0101_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta.transdecoder.pep
│   ├── JCV23-0101_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta.transdecoder.cds
│   ├── JCV23-0101_Viridiplantae_filtered.fasta.transdecoder.gff3
│   └── ...
├── JCV23-0102_20250617_transdecoder_out/
├── JCV23-0201_20250617_transdecoder_out/
└── ...

The predicted protein sequences generated by TransDecoder constitute the final coding-sequence dataset and serve as the primary input for functional annotation, orthology inference, and comparative transcriptomic analyses.

17 TransDecoder Analysis of the Global Trinity Assembly

In addition to the per-sample analyses, TransDecoder was also applied to the global transcriptome assembly generated from all RNA-seq samples combined. This approach allows the identification of coding regions from the complete transcriptome dataset and provides a comprehensive protein-coding reference for downstream analyses.

The input transcriptome corresponded to the global Trinity assembly generated from all cleaned reads:

03_trinity_separated/Zamia_all_20250902_trinity/

The predicted coding sequences were stored in:

18_B_TransDecoder_All_Zamia_Trinity/

17.1 Purpose of the global analysis

Running TransDecoder on the combined assembly offers several advantages:

  • Identification of coding regions from the complete transcriptome.
  • Recovery of transcripts that may be absent or fragmented in individual assemblies.
  • Generation of a comprehensive protein dataset for functional annotation.
  • Creation of a reference protein catalog for comparative and evolutionary analyses.

17.2 TransDecoder workflow

The analysis followed the standard two-step TransDecoder workflow.

17.2.1 TransDecoder.LongOrfs

Identifies candidate open reading frames (ORFs) from the assembled transcripts.

17.2.2 TransDecoder.Predict

Evaluates the candidate ORFs and predicts the most likely protein-coding sequences.

17.3 Summary statistics generated

After ORF prediction, a summary table was automatically generated containing:

  • Total number of predicted ORFs.
  • Minimum protein length.
  • Maximum protein length.
  • Average protein length.

These metrics provide a general overview of the coding potential recovered from the global transcriptome assembly.

17.4 SLURM script used for global TransDecoder analysis

SLURM script: 17_B_TransDecoder_ALLZamia_trinity_RNA.sh 
#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J Tdeco-Allzamia
#SBATCH -o TransDecoder_AllZam.out
#SBATCH -p medium
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=5
#SBATCH --mem=30G
#SBATCH --time=7-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca


##############################################

module load transdecoder/5.5.0
module load usearch/9.2.64_i86linux32

##############################################
# 1. Rutas
##############################################

base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
fasta_global="${base_dir}/03_trinity_separated/Zamia_all_20250902_trinity/Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta"
out_dir="${base_dir}/18_B_TransDecoder_All_Zamia_Trinity"

mkdir -p "$out_dir"
cd "$out_dir" || exit 1

##############################################
# 2. Ejecutar TransDecoder
##############################################

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] Iniciando TransDecoder para FASTA global"

# Paso 1: Long ORFs
TransDecoder.LongOrfs -t "$fasta_global"

# Paso 2: Predict
TransDecoder.Predict -t "$fasta_global"

echo "[$(date +'%F %T')] TransDecoder finalizado."

##############################################
# 3. Estad sticas del .pep
##############################################

pep_file="$(basename "$fasta_global").transdecoder.pep"
resumen_out="${out_dir}/resumen_transdecoder.tsv"

if [[ ! -f "$pep_file" ]]; then
    echo "[ERROR] No se encontr  el archivo .pep"
    exit 1
fi

lens=($(awk '/^>/ {if (seq) print length(seq); seq=""} !/^>/ {seq=seq $0} END{print length(seq)}' "$pep_file"))

total=${#lens[@]}
min=${lens[0]}
max=${lens[0]}
sum=0

for len in "${lens[@]}"; do
    (( sum += len ))
    (( len < min )) && min=$len
    (( len > max )) && max=$len
done

avg=$(awk -v s=$sum -v t=$total 'BEGIN{printf "%.2f", s/t}')

echo -e "total_orfs\tmin_len\tmax_len\tavg_len" > "$resumen_out"
echo -e "${total}\t${min}\t${max}\t${avg}" >> "$resumen_out"

echo "? Tabla resumen generada: $resumen_out"

17.5 Output files

18_B_TransDecoder_All_Zamia_Trinity/
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.pep
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.cds
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.gff3
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.bed
├── longest_orfs.pep
├── longest_orfs.cds
├── resumo_transdecoder.tsv
└── ...

17.5.1 Description of the main output files

File Description
.pep Predicted protein sequences translated from candidate ORFs
.cds Coding nucleotide sequences corresponding to predicted proteins
.gff3 Coordinates of predicted ORFs within transcripts
.bed BED-format coordinates of coding regions
resumen_transdecoder.tsv Summary statistics of predicted proteins

The resulting protein dataset represents the predicted coding component of the complete Zamia transcriptome and can be used as input for functional annotation pipelines such as Trinotate, EggNOG, InterProScan, or comparative genomics analyses.

18 Trinotate: Functional Annotation of the Global Transcriptome

Trinotate is a comprehensive annotation framework designed to provide functional characterization of transcriptome assemblies. It integrates sequence similarity searches, protein domain identification, signal peptide prediction, Gene Ontology (GO) terms, and functional databases into a single SQLite database and annotation report.

Unlike the previous analyses, which were executed on the HPC cluster, the Trinotate workflow was performed locally on a workstation using the official Docker image provided by the Trinotate developers. This approach simplifies software installation and ensures compatibility with all required dependencies.

For additional information, see:

  • Trinotate Wiki: https://github.com/Trinotate/Trinotate/wiki
  • Trinotate via Docker: https://github.com/Trinotate/Trinotate/wiki/TrinotateViaDocker

18.1 Input files

The annotation was performed using the global Trinity assembly and the predicted coding sequences generated by TransDecoder.

Input files
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.gene_trans_map
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.pep
├── uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastx.outfmt6
├── uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastp.outfmt6
├── TrinotatePFAM_B_domtblout
└── signalp.out

18.2 Trinotate databases and annotation sources

Trinotate integrates several complementary sources of biological information:

Resource Description
SwissProt BLASTX Similarity between transcripts and curated protein sequences.
SwissProt BLASTP Similarity between predicted proteins and curated protein sequences.
Pfam Conserved protein domains identified with HMMER.
SignalP Prediction of secreted proteins and signal peptides.
Gene Ontology (GO) Functional annotation of biological processes, molecular functions, and cellular components.
EggNOG Orthology and functional classification database.

18.3 Docker environment

The official Docker image was used to execute Trinotate and maintain a reproducible environment.

docker run -it --rm \
  -v "Trinotate-v4.0.2:/usr/local/src/Trinotate" \
  -v "databases:/databases" \
  -v "ZamiaAll_trinity:/data" \
  trinityrnaseq/trinotate:latest /bin/bash

18.4 Creation of the Trinotate database

A local SQLite database was generated using the most recent Trinotate release (v4.0.2).

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db /data/Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --create \
  --trinotate_data_dir /databases

18.4.1 EggNOG database issue

During database construction, the automatic EggNOG download failed because the URL used by Trinotate was no longer active. To resolve this issue:

  1. The EggNOG annotation file was downloaded manually.
  2. The corresponding checkpoint file was generated.
  3. The download command inside Build_Trinotate_Boilerplate_SQLite_db.pl was temporarily disabled. Line # \(pipeliner->add_commands(new Command("wget \"\)EGGNOG_DAT_URL" -O NOG.annotations.tsv.gz”, “eggnog_download.ok”) );
  4. The database creation process was resumed.

This modification allowed successful completion of the SQLite database construction.

18.5 Initialization of the transcriptome database

The Trinity assembly, transcript-to-gene map, and predicted proteins were loaded into the newly created Trinotate database.

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --init \
  --gene_trans_map Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.gene_trans_map \
  --transcript_fasta Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta \
  --transdecoder_pep Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.pep

18.6 Loading functional annotation results

The different annotation datasets were incorporated into the SQLite database.

18.6.1 BLASTX against SwissProt

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --LOAD_swissprot_blastx \
  uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastx.outfmt6

Number of BLASTX annotations loaded:

235,669 hits

18.6.2 BLASTP against SwissProt

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --LOAD_swissprot_blastp \
  uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastp.outfmt6

Number of BLASTP annotations loaded:

321,403 hits

18.6.3 Pfam domains

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --LOAD_pfam \
  TrinotatePFAM_B_domtblout

Number of Pfam domain annotations:

386 hits

18.6.4 SignalP predictions

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --LOAD_signalp \
  signalp.out

Number of SignalP annotations:

4,166 hits

18.6.5 EggNOG annotations

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --LOAD_EggnogMapper \
  NOG.annotations.tsv.gz

Although the EggNOG table was successfully loaded, only a limited number of entries were recovered. Additional annotation using the current version of EggNOG-mapper may improve functional coverage.

18.7 Gene Ontology verification

The Gene Ontology tables were successfully populated during database creation.

sqlite3 Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM go;"

sqlite3 Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM go_slim_mapping;"

Results:

GO terms:               48,251
GO slim mappings:       35,338

These values indicate that GO annotations were correctly incorporated into the Trinotate database and are available for downstream functional analyses.

18.8 Generation of the final annotation report

The final annotation report was generated directly from the SQLite database.

/usr/local/src/Trinotate/Trinotate \
  --db /data/Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite \
  --report \
  --incl_pep \
  --incl_trans \
  > /data/Trinotate_FINAL_04Mar2026.xls

18.9 Output files

16_Trinotate_Zamia_RNA/
├── Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite
├── Trinotate_FINAL_04Mar2026.xls
├── uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastx.outfmt6
├── uniprot_sprot.ncbi.blastp.outfmt6
├── TrinotatePFAM_B_domtblout
├── signalp.out
└── annotation_logs/

The final Trinotate report integrates sequence similarity, protein domains, signal peptide predictions, orthology information, and Gene Ontology assignments, providing a comprehensive functional annotation resource for the global Zamia transcriptome.

19 Salmon: Transcript Abundance Estimation

Salmon is a fast and accurate tool for transcript quantification from RNA-seq data. It estimates transcript abundance by quasi-mapping sequencing reads to a reference transcriptome and reports normalized expression values such as TPM (Transcripts Per Million).

In this analysis, transcript abundance was estimated by mapping each sample against the global Trinity assembly generated from all Zamia samples. This approach allows expression values to be directly compared across samples because all reads are quantified against the same transcript reference.

For more information, see:

  • Salmon: https://combine-lab.github.io/salmon/

19.1 Input files

The analysis used the global Trinity assembly as the reference transcriptome and the cleaned paired-end reads generated after Trimmomatic filtering.

Input files
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta
├── Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.gene_trans_map
├── sample_R1_paired.fastq.gz
└── sample_R2_paired.fastq.gz

19.2 Output directory

All abundance estimates and expression matrices were stored in:

20_B_TPM_abun_Zamia_trinity/

19.3 Salmon workflow

The workflow consisted of three main steps:

  1. Construction of a Salmon index from the global Trinity assembly.
  2. Quantification of transcript abundance for each sample independently.
  3. Generation of transcript-level and gene-level abundance matrices across all samples.

19.4 Important parameters

The following parameters were used during quantification:

Parameter Description
salmon index Builds an index of the transcriptome assembly.
-k 31 K-mer size used for indexing.
-l A Automatically detects library type.
-1 / -2 Paired-end read files.
-p 8 Uses 8 CPU threads.
--validateMappings Improves mapping accuracy by validating candidate alignments.

These parameters provide a balance between computational efficiency and quantification accuracy.

19.5 SLURM script used for Salmon

#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -J salmon
#SBATCH -o salmon-%j.out
#SBATCH -p medium
#SBATCH --array=2-12%1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=50G
#SBATCH --time=7-00:00:00
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#SBATCH --mail-user=gfpeb@ulaval.ca


#############################################
# 1. Activar entorno
#############################################

module load python/3.6
module load htslib/1.8
module load samtools/1.13
module load bowtie/2.4.4
module load jellyfish/2.3.0
module load salmon/1.10.0
module load java/jdk/1.8.0_102
module load trinity/2.15.2
module load rnammer/1.2
module load tmhmm/2.0c
module load signalp/4.1
module load hmmer/3.3
module load ncbiblast/2.10.0
module load transdecoder/5.5.0
module load trinotate/3.2.0


#=================== 2. Rutas base ===================
base_dir="/project/jcvaguilar/users/jcvil9/Gab_Zamia_Transcri"
reads_base="${base_dir}/01_clean_reads_Zamia_RNA_trimmomatic"

# Archivos originales (Trinity global)
assembly="${base_dir}/03_trinity_separated/Zamia_all_20250902_trinity/Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta"
gene_map="${base_dir}/03_trinity_separated/Zamia_all_20250902_trinity/Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.gene_trans_map"

# Carpeta de trabajo
output_base="${base_dir}/20_B_TPM_abun_Zamia_trinity"
mkdir -p "$output_base"

#=================== 3. Crear  indice Salmon ===================
index_dir="${output_base}/salmon_index"
if [[ ! -f "${index_dir}/versionInfo.json" ]]; then
   echo "Creando  ndice Salmon en $index_dir"
   mkdir -p "$index_dir"
   salmon index -t "$assembly" -i "$index_dir" -k 31
fi

#=================== 4. Seleccionar muestra ===================
samples_list="${base_dir}/samples_list.txt"
sample=$(sed -n "${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID}p" "$samples_list" | tr -d '[:space:]')
echo "Procesando muestra: $sample"

left_reads="${reads_base}/${sample}/${sample}_R1_paired.fastq.gz"
right_reads="${reads_base}/${sample}/${sample}_R2_paired.fastq.gz"

out_dir="${output_base}/${sample}_salmon"
mkdir -p "$out_dir"

#=================== 5. Ejecutar Salmon ===================
salmon quant \
   -i "$index_dir" \
   -l A \
   -1 "$left_reads" -2 "$right_reads" \
   -p $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK \
   -o "$out_dir" \
   --validateMappings

echo "Finalizado Salmon para $sample"

#=================== 6. Generar matrices ===================
if [[ ${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID} -eq $(wc -l < "$samples_list") ]]; then
   echo "Generando matrices de abundancia..."
   /prg/trinityrnaseq/2.15.2/util/abundance_estimates_to_matrix.pl \
     --est_method salmon \
     --out_prefix ${output_base}/Zamia_all_TPM \
     --name_sample_by_basedir \
     --gene_trans_map "$gene_map" \
     $(find ${output_base} -name "quant.sf")

   echo "Matrices guardadas en ${output_base}/Zamia_all_TPM.*"
fi

19.6 Generation of abundance matrices

After all samples were quantified, Trinity’s utility script abundance_estimates_to_matrix.pl was used to combine the individual quant.sf files into expression matrices.

Two types of abundance matrices were generated:

Matrix Description
Transcript matrix TPM estimates for individual transcript isoforms.
Gene matrix TPM estimates aggregated at the gene level using the Trinity gene-transcript map.

These matrices can be used for downstream analyses such as differential expression, clustering, PCA, co-expression networks, and functional enrichment.

19.7 Output files

For each sample, Salmon generates a quantification directory containing abundance estimates.

20_B_TPM_abun_Zamia_trinity/
├── salmon_index/
│
├── JCV23-0101_salmon/
│   ├── quant.sf
│   ├── cmd_info.json
│   ├── lib_format_counts.json
│   └── logs/
│
├── JCV23-0102_salmon/
├── JCV23-0201_salmon/
├── ...
│
├── Zamia_all_TPM.isoform.counts.matrix
├── Zamia_all_TPM.isoform.TPM.not_cross_norm
├── Zamia_all_TPM.gene.counts.matrix
└── Zamia_all_TPM.gene.TPM.not_cross_norm

19.8 Interpretation of the main output files

File Description
quant.sf Transcript abundance estimates for each sample.
isoform.counts.matrix Raw counts at transcript level.
isoform.TPM.not_cross_norm TPM-normalized transcript abundance matrix.
gene.counts.matrix Raw counts aggregated by gene.
gene.TPM.not_cross_norm TPM-normalized abundance matrix at gene level.

The TPM matrices generated by Salmon constitute the primary dataset used for downstream transcriptomic analyses because they provide normalized estimates of transcript abundance that are comparable among samples.

20 Final Remarks and Future Analyses

This report describes the complete workflow used to generate, evaluate, filter, annotate, and quantify the Zamia transcriptome. Through successive quality-control and contamination-filtering steps, a high-confidence reference transcriptome was obtained and functionally annotated.

Two key products of this pipeline will serve as the foundation for downstream biological analyses:

20.1 Reference Transcriptome

The final transcriptome assembly generated with Trinity and subsequently filtered using coverage and taxonomic criteria provides a curated set of transcript sequences representative of the Zamia transcriptome.

The principal files include:

Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta
Zamia_all_20250902.Trinity.fasta.transdecoder.pep
Trinotate_FINAL_04Mar2026.xls
Trinotate_03Mar2026.sqlite

These resources contain the assembled transcripts, predicted coding sequences, and functional annotations required for gene characterization and biological interpretation.

20.2 Transcript Abundance Matrices

Transcript abundance was estimated using Salmon by mapping each sample against the global Trinity assembly.

The principal output files include:

Zamia_all_TPM.isoform.counts.matrix
Zamia_all_TPM.isoform.TPM.not_cross_norm
Zamia_all_TPM.gene.counts.matrix
Zamia_all_TPM.gene.TPM.not_cross_norm

These matrices contain expression estimates at both transcript and gene levels and represent the primary dataset for quantitative transcriptomic analyses.

20.3 Future Analyses

The transcriptome assembly, functional annotation database, and expression matrices generated in this workflow will be used in subsequent analyses, including:

  • Differential gene expression analysis between experimental conditions or biological groups.
  • Identification of significantly upregulated and downregulated genes.
  • Functional enrichment analyses using Gene Ontology (GO) categories.
  • Pathway enrichment analyses based on functional annotations recovered through Trinotate.
  • Exploration of biological processes, molecular functions, and cellular components associated with transcriptional responses.
  • Identification of candidate genes potentially involved in physiological, developmental, or adaptive processes in Zamia.

Together, the Trinity assembly, Trinotate annotation, and Salmon abundance estimates provide a robust framework for investigating gene expression patterns and their biological significance in future transcriptomic studies.
